It doesn’t mean what you think it means
If you ask most JavaScript developers what const does, you'll get some version of the same answer:
“It means the value can never change.”
It’s a reasonable guess. It’s also wrong — and this one misconception trips up developers at every level, from bootcamp grads to senior engineers who’ve just never had a reason to question it.
Here’s the actual rule:
const locks the variable. Not the value.
Let’s break down what that really means.
What const Actually Does
const prevents you from reassigning the variable — that is, pointing it at something new. That's it. That's the whole promise it makes.
Whether you can change what’s inside the value depends entirely on the type of that value.
Primitives: locked down
Numbers, strings, and booleans are stored by value. When you declare one with const, you're locking that exact value in place.
const age = 25;
age = 26; // ❌ TypeError: Assignment to constant variable.No surprises here. This is the behavior most people expect — and it’s the reason the misconception spreads. The intuition works fine for primitives, so nobody questions it further.
Objects and arrays: wide open
Objects and arrays are stored by reference. const only locks the reference — the pointer to the object in memory — not the contents of the object itself.
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // ✅ Totally legal
console.log(arr); // [1, 2, 3, 4]const user = { name: "Ali" };
user.name = "Sara"; // ✅ Totally legal
console.log(user); // { name: "Sara" }Both of these run without a single error. The array grew. The object’s property changed. const didn't stop either one, because neither operation touched the reference — only the data the reference points to.
What const would stop is this:
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr = [4, 5, 6]; // ❌ Error — this reassigns the referenceThat’s the line const actually guards.
Why This Happens: Value vs. Reference
The mental model that clears this up is understanding how JavaScript stores data.

• Primitives (number, string, boolean, etc.) are stored directly in the variable. const freezes that value in place because there's nothing else to point to.

• Objects and arrays are stored in memory, and the variable just holds a reference — an address pointing to that memory location. const freezes the address, not what lives there.
So when you mutate an object or push to an array, you’re not touching the reference at all. You’re reaching through it and modifying the data on the other side. As far as const is concerned, nothing about the variable itself changed — it still points to the exact same place it always did.
This is the core distinction: const prevents reassignment, not mutation.
Want Actual Immutability?
If you genuinely need to lock down an object’s contents, const alone won't do it. You need Object.freeze():
const user = Object.freeze({ name: "Ali" });
user.name = "Sara"; // Silently fails (or throws in strict mode)
console.log(user.name); // "Ali"One catch worth knowing: Object.freeze() is shallow. It locks the top-level properties, but nested objects inside are still fully mutable:
const user = Object.freeze({
name: "Ali",
address: { city: "Lahore" }
});user.address.city = "Karachi"; // ✅ Still legal — nested object isn't frozen
console.log(user.address.city); // "Karachi"For deep immutability, you’d need to recursively freeze every nested object, or reach for a library that handles it for you.
Why This Matters in Practice
This isn’t just trivia — it has real consequences in day-to-day code:

• React state bugs: Directly mutating a const state object instead of creating a new one is one of the most common reasons components silently fail to re-render.

• Accidental side effects: Passing a const object into a function and assuming it's "safe" because it's const — when the function mutates it anyway.

• False sense of security in code reviews: Seeing const and assuming immutability, when the object underneath is being reshaped elsewhere in the codebase.
Once you internalize that const is about the binding, not the data, a lot of these bugs become obvious before they even happen.
The Takeaway
const isn't a promise that your data won't change. It's a promise that your variable won't point to something else. For primitives, those two things happen to look the same. For objects and arrays, they're completely different guarantees — and confusing the two is where the bugs creep in.
If you want real immutability, reach for Object.freeze() (and remember it's shallow), or use immutable data patterns intentionally rather than assuming const gives them to you for free.

Did you already know this, or did something click just now? Let me know in the comments — and if you found this useful, follow for more JavaScript deep dives.
